data.title: Hama Intelligence Readies U.S. IPO Plan
data.description:
Hama Is Growing, But Valuation Is High

Hama Intelligence Limited (HAMA) has filed to raise $6.6 million in an IPO of its Class A ordinary shares, according to SEC F-1/A registration information.

HAMA provides various corporate and business advisory services in Hong Kong and Singapore.

The company is growing revenue from a tiny base but has numerous risks and extreme valuation assumptions for the IPO.

My outlook on the HAMA IPO is to sell (avoid it).

What Does Hama Do?

Singapore-based Hama Intelligence Limited was founded to develop various business financial and advisory service offerings for small and medium-sized firms.

Management is led by Chief Executive Officer Mr. Wai Ting Cheung, who has been with the firm since 2025 and was previously director and responsible officer of Seeds International Asset Management.

The company's primary offerings include:

  • One-stop corporate services.

  • Business financial consulting.

  • Accounting.

  • Internal controls.

  • Tax and compliance advisory.

  • Investor relationship management.

HAMA has booked a fair market value investment of approximately $185,000 in debt as of February 28, 2025, from investor Mr. Wing Sum Ho.

The company's revenue contribution by segment and timing is shown in the pie charts below, with Business & Financial Consulting generating the most revenue by segment:

Employee & Compensation expenses as a percentage of total revenue have fallen as revenues have risen, as the figures below indicate:

Employee & Compensation

(Source: SEC).

The Employee & Compensation efficiency multiple, i.e., how many dollars of additional revenue are produced from each dollar of Employee & Compensation expense, was 2.2x in the most recent reporting period. (Source: SEC).

What Is Hama's Market?

According to a 2025 market research report by Mordor Intelligence, the market in Hong Kong for management consulting services is approximately $1 billion in 2025 and is expected to reach $1.3 billion by 2030.

This represents a projected CAGR of around 6% from 2026 to 2030, a moderate expected growth rate.

The primary factors driving this expected growth are cross-border complexities between Hong Kong and mainland China and a growing strategic significance of the Greater Bay Area region.

Also, some drivers of expected growth of the market in the greater Hong Kong area are described in the table below:

The company faces a fragmented and highly competitive market with low barriers to entry and competitors include other corporate consultancy firms and professional service firms of various sizes and degrees of specialization.

The firm pursues clients from among small and medium-sized businesses for its full range of corporate service offerings.

HAMA targets businesses located in Hong Kong and throughout the Greater Bay Area region of Guangzhou-Shenzhen-Hong Kong-Macau-Zhuhai.

I've prepared a thumbnail SWOT analysis for HAMA:

Strengths

  • Broad service offerings and ability to cross-sell, diverse client base, and lower regulatory risk.

Weaknesses

  • No long-term customer contracts, low switching costs, reliance on a non-US auditor.

Opportunities

  • Geographic expansion in Southeast Asia, growing regulatory complexity, and leveraging technology and automation (Generative AI) to scale more efficiently.

Threats

  • Chinese regulatory and political risks and wide competition with low barriers to entry.

Hama's Recent Financial Results

The firm's recent financial performance is summarized as shown below:

  • Increasing topline revenue from a small base.

  • Higher gross profit and gross margin.

  • Growing operating profit and cash flow from operations.

Hama's IPO Plan

HAMA intends to sell 1.1 million shares of Class A ordinary shares at a proposed midpoint price of $6.00 per share for gross proceeds of approximately $6.6 million, not including the sale of customary underwriter options.

No existing or new shareholders have indicated an interest in acquiring additional shares of the IPO, as described in the prospectus.

Class A stockholders will receive one vote per share.

Class B votes will have twenty (20) votes per share and the company will be a 'controlled company' immediately post-IPO.

Company, shareholder and executive lockups will be for the typical 180-day period following the effective date of the prospectus.

Dilution in net tangible book value per share for investors in the IPO is expected to be $5.71, which is within the typical range for a company at IPO.

The company's enterprise value at IPO (assuming no underwriter options) will approximate $115 million.

The float-to-outstanding-shares ratio (excluding underwriter options) will be approximately 5.47%, so the stock will be a 'low float' stock subject to higher volatility in open market trading.

According to HAMA's most recent regulatory filing, it plans to use the net proceeds as follows:

approximately 30% for business development and marketing;

approximately 30% for expanding our local operations and setting up overseas branches; and approximately 40% for general administration and working capital.

(Source: SEC).

The firm's presentation of the company roadshow isn't available.

Pertaining to potential legal exposure, leadership says the company is not currently the target of legal actions that would have a material adverse effect on its financial condition or operations.

Listed underwriters of the IPO are Pacific Century Securities

Hama Is Growing And Profitable But Faces Risks

Hama is seeking US public capital market funding before the US market becomes more difficult for small Asian IPOs.

Hama's financials have shown growing topline revenue from a small base, increased gross profit and gross margin and higher operating profit and cash flow from operations.

Free cash flow for the twelve months ended February 28, 2025, was approximately $1.0 million.

Employee & Compensation expenses as a percentage of total revenue fell as revenue grew and its Employee & Compensation efficiency multiple was 2.2x in the most recent fiscal year.

The firm currently plans to pay no dividends in the foreseeable future and will likely retain any future earnings for reinvestment in its growth plans and working capital needs.

Hama's recent capital spending history shows it has spent lightly on capital expenditures as a percentage of its operating cash flow.

The firm's growth strategy includes expanding into other Southeast Asian markets, pursuing selective acquisitions, improving operating leverage through Gen AI tools and other automations, and cross-selling services to clients.

The market opportunity for providing corporate advisory and business services is large and expected to grow at only a moderate rate of growth and features intense competition from market players of all sizes.

Risks to the company's outlook as a public firm include its 'foreign private issuer' and 'emerging growth company' status, which enables management to divulge less information to shareholders.

Many of these types of Asian companies have performed unpredictably in open market trading, with most providing poor financial returns to investors.

Nasdaq is also in the process of increasing minimum IPO size and implementing other hurdles that will likely sharply reduce these small-cap IPOs.

The firm has geographic concentration risks due to its focus on Hong Kong and does not have long-term binding contracts with its clients, leading to the risk of client switching or loss.

As to valuation, HAMA is seeking an EV/Revenue multiple of 63x and an EV/EBITDA multiple of 92x, both of which are extreme and unwarranted.

data.title: AbCellera: Shares Have Momentum, But Does The Pipeline? It's Too Early To Tell
data.description:
Investment Overview

I have been quite critical of AbCellera's (NASDAQ:ABCL) business in notes for Seeking Alpha in the past - awarding its stock a sell rating in March 2023, followed by three hold ratings in December 2023, May 2024, and March this year, so it is only fair to lavish some praise on the company given its share price is up >135% since my last hold call - although it remains down nearly 20% since my original sell call.

AbCellera is a Vancouver, British Columbia based biotech company that, according to its Q2 2025 quarterly report / 10Q filing is focused on "the development of antibody-based drugs", and is "committed to improving discovery and development". In my last post I discussed how:

AbCellera was able to develop 2 COVID anti-virals alongside partner Eli Lilly (LLY) in bamlanivimab and bebtelovimab, which helped it post revenues of $233m in 2020, $375.2m in 2021, and $485.4m in 2022, before the drug was withdrawn from the market as it was no longer effective against newer strains of COVID.

Since then, it has been slim pickings for AbCellera, revenues-wise - $38m in 2023, and $28.8m in 2024, while combined operating income of ~$225m in 2021/22, has become a combined net loss of >$(520m) in 2023/24.

The company's IPO in December 2020 raised ~$483m, at $20 per share, and stock currently trades at $6.5 per share, so performance has been underwhelming, however shares traded <$1 in May this year, and are up >195% on a six-month basis.

I had been critical of AbCellera's business model - working with pharma partners and attempting to discover the next bamlanivimab and bebtelovimab, in exchange for milestone payments and royalties - in previous notes, suggesting that with such a model, AbCellera takes on the all of the risks of early stage drug discovery - the proverbial search for a needle in a haystack - while allowing partners to reap most of the reward - potentially, billions of dollars of commercial revenues, while AbCellera earns perhaps a few hundred million in milestones, plus a single digit percentage royalty stream.

To its credit, management appears to have rapidly pivoted its business to in-house drug development (if you have the tools and believe in them, why not build wholly owned programs with them?), as we can see in the slide below (taken from a recent corporate presentation):

AbCellera - Internal Pipeline Overview - Phase 1 Data Catalysts In 2026

AbCellera expects to share Phase 1 data from studies of both of its lead candidates, ABCL635, and ABCL575 in 2026.

ABCL635 is described as "a potential first-in-class antibody for the non-hormonal treatment of vasomotor symptoms (hot flashes)", that targets the neurokonin 3 receptor, which is a G protein-coupled receptor ("GPCR"), and targets a disease with an addressable market of ~40m women of menopausal age in the US, 30% of whom experience moderate-to-severe VMS, AbCellera's research suggests.

ABCL635 is dosed monthly, and subcutaneously, which AbCellera hopes will be a competitive advantage over current standards of care, the main one being menopause hormone therapy ("MHT"). AbCellera says that ~12% of women are contraindicated for MHT, however, and cites research suggesting 57% of women are against using MHT.

AbCellera also points to the fact that Astellas Pharma's Veozah (fezolinetant) and Bayer's Lynkuet (elinzanetant) - the first having been approved in the US in 2023 to treat hot flashes, and the second under regulatory review - are both small molecule NK3R antagonists, as evidence its drug can be successful, although presumably these two drugs are also its direct rivals for market share, if approval is secured one day.

As mentioned, ABCL635 is a monthly injectable as opposed to a daily oral drug like Veozah and Lynkuet, and an antibody-based therapeutic as opposed to a small molecule, and not therefore associated with liver toxicity. Stock has been climbing ever since AbCellera announced it had obtained authorisation from Health Canada to initiate a Phase 1 study of the drug.

On May 30th this year, also doubtless contributing to recent gains, AbCellera announced that it had dosed a first patient in a Phase 1 study of its second internal candidate, ABCL-575, an OX40L antagonist - a similar mechanism of action ("MoA") as a candidate being developed by Pharma giant Sanofi (SNY), amlitelimab, which recently aced a Phase 3 study in atopic dermatitis ("AD"), and Amgen's (AMGN) rocatilimab, also in Phase 3 studies in AD. The advantage of ABCL-575 appears to be its potential once-every six-month dosing regime.

Regarding ABCL635, AbCellera's CEO Carl Hansen told analysts on its Q2 earnings call:

we believe the main scientific risk for ABCL635 is whether or not we can achieve sufficient target engagement. We expect this will be addressed through biomarker and proof-of-concept studies that are part of our Phase I design. As previously stated, we expect initial safety and efficacy data from this trial in mid-2026.

And regarding, ABCL-575 the CEO commented:

We recently presented preclinical data, which demonstrates it has potent functional activity in vitro that is in line with Amlitelimab as measured by cytokine responses across a variety of cytokines, including IL-13, IL-5 and IL-9.

As a reminder, ABCL575 is engineered with half-life extension to support less frequent dosing. Based on PK data we have obtained from studies in FcRn humanized mice, we predict a human half-life of approximately 67 days.

AbCellera is also hoping to secure an Investigational New Drug ("IND") approval (which allows the company to initiate a Phase 1 study) for a third candidate, ABCL688, an ion channel- or GPCR-targeted antibody development candidate focused on autoimmune diseases. The IND submission has been promised for mid-2026.

Royalty & Partnered Programs

Given AbCellera has spent the past ten years perfecting its drug development technology platform, and reported a cash position of $553m as of the end of Q2, it makes sense to use it at full capacity, and the company still has plenty of partnered programs in development.

AbCellera says it has 96 partner initiated programs with "downstream participation", with 16 molecules in the clinic, with a mean royalty rate of 3.2%, and 37 "understood to be progressing" (source: investor presentation). AbCellera shares the below overview of programs, which in my view is not especially compelling, although there does appear to be three oncology programs in the clinic and active, alongside a single neurosicence candidate:

Analysis - Stock Has Momentum, But Does Company?

AbCellera's market cap today stands at $1.78bn, which seems fairly high for a biotech with just two Phase 1 stage drug candidates, and a handful of partnered assets in the clinic.

Stock has been soaring in value, however - up >25% in the last week, although it is difficult to find specific reasons why. The Sanofi amlitelimab data may have helped boost optimism around the 575 program, and the market may be celebrating the fact that AbCellera is now entering the clinic with two assets of its own, both of which have potentially unique advantages over the competition.

With that said, however, AbCellera still burns through cash fairly rapidly - net loss in 1H26 was $(148.35m) - and it is years behind its competition in both the fields of hot flashes and atopic dermatitis - its assets have proven nothing in the clinic as yet.

Still, if we consider the long-term, the market clearly seems to believe AbCellera can play the long game and win. It's not always easy for a layman to look at a biotech's preclinical work and predict a path to success for a drug candidate, or to evaluate the promise of one manufacturing plant or technology platform over another, but clearly, AbCellera's efforts have some support. The likes of VC firms Baker Brothers, Baillie Gifford and Blackrock are all invested in AbCellera stock.

It can certainly help a biotech to have influential contacts in the world of finance, and in AbCellera's case, it may also help to have patient ones, because, two Phase 1 data readouts and an IND submission aside, there are few investment highlights / catalysts expected this year, or next.

Concluding Thoughts - Does AbCellera Stock Warrant An Upgrade, or Downgrade From Current Hold Rating?

This is tricky question to answer because, as mentioned, by most traditional measures its hard to justify AbCellera's current valuation - a biotech with two Phase 1 stage assets whose MoAs are similar to drugs close to being approved would not normally warrant a market cap valuation in the triple-digit-millions, in my experience, let alone in the billions.

Clearly, however, the market is showing a lot of faith in AbCellera, and if we home in on the drug candidates, perhaps their unique competitive advantages - monthly subcutaneous dosing, potential for better safety, longer half-lives, large market opportunities - are more compelling than they seem.

I think it's interesting to note that AbCellera states in its presentation that, in relation to ABCL-635, that:

Increasing use of GLP-1 agonists is significantly increasing the autoinjector-experienced population

According to an abstract published to Nature.com

The glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor, known as GLP-1R, is a vital component of the G protein-coupled receptor (GPCR) family and is found primarily on the surfaces of various cell types within the human body

The GLP-1 drug class has taken the pharmaceutical world by storm over the past few years, with the advent of Novo Nordisk's (NVO) and Eli Lilly's (LLY) "miracle" weight loss and type 2 diabetes drugs semaglutide and tirzepatide, or Wegovy / Ozempic, and Zepbound / Mounjaro, which have quickly become the second and third best-selling prescription drugs in circulation, both earning >$8bn of revenues in the last quarter alone.

By playing in the GCPR space, AbCellera is potentially lining up to one day play in the GLP-1 space, which could result in its developing new drug candidates with massive market potential. With that said, the likes of Septerna (SEPN), Terns Pharma (TERN), and Altimmune (ALT) are all in the GLP-1 space, and none enjoy a >$1bn market cap, although Pfizer (PFE) did agree to acquire GLP-1 developed Metsera in September, in a deal worth an initial $4.9bn.

At this moment in time, I can't upgrade AbCellera stock to a "Buy" because objectively speaking, at this time if I apply the usual risk factors - length of time to any potential drug approval, phase of development, financial strength, track record - they suggest AbCellera stock is significantly overvalued.

Nevertheless, my interest is piqued by the recent run-up in the share price and the projects the company may work on in the future, and what its drug discovery engine may be capable of in the future - AbCellera is already talking up a fourth drug candidate it expects to unveil soon - could it be a GLP-1 receptor agonist?

It doesn't necessarily have to be, to be considered a very valuable drug - in fact, the OX40 targeting ABCL-575 candidate would be a multi-billion selling asset if it showed itself to be superior to current AD drugs, such as Sanofi's >$14bn per annum selling Dupixent.

There is definitely hope for AbCellera's future, then, but in my view, I can't see how the two upcoming Phase 1 data readouts could be compelling enough to send the company's market cap any higher, given the early stage of development, so am on the side-lines until there is more concrete evidence that the company has a best-in-class asset in its stable.

data.title: Tesla: The Q3 Delivery Record Doesn't Change My Outlook
data.description:
Q3 2025 was a very important quarter in the U.S. electric vehicle industry. With Donald Trump becoming President again earlier in the year, the Republican-led government approved the elimination of the $7,500 tax credit for EVs. U.S. sales leader Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA) reported its Q3 production and delivery figures on Thursday morning, with the numbers expected to be quite good as consumers raced to beat the expiration deadline. Tesla certainly took advantage of the expiring credit, reporting a quarterly record for vehicle deliveries.

Previous coverage of the name

The last time I covered Tesla was back in late July, when the company announced a mostly poor Q2 earnings report. Revenues beat Street estimates, but not as many gains were shown on the bottom line. Management also pulled its guidance for vehicle delivery growth this year, and the company maintained a very strong balance sheet. Since that time, shares have surged more than 48% with U.S. markets racing to all-time highs, but the S&P (SP500) has only increased a little more than 5% since.

The Q3 production and delivery report

There were multiple items expected to help Tesla's delivery figures during the third quarter. First, the obvious U.S. tailwind from the expiration of the tax credit. Second, Tesla launched a new long-range Model 3 and an extended-length, six-seater Model Y in China. Tesla has also generally done better with its deliveries in the second half of the year. In the graphic below, you can see how things evolved from Q2 2025 to Q3 2025.

The Tesla investor relations compiled Street estimate was just over 443,000. This number was sent out almost a week before Tesla actually reported its number, so there were still some analysts hiking their estimates into the actual report. I was expecting a little less than 475,000 for the quarter, so Tesla certainly delivered well above expectations. This could mark the top for deliveries for a time, especially given the seasonality usually seen in winter months for early 2026.

The company also announced a record 12.5 GWh of energy storage deployments, although I would have expected a little bit more given that the Shanghai Megapack factory opened earlier this year plus the production ramp from its U.S. facilities. Tesla will report its full Q3 results on October 22nd after the close, at which point we'll see how all of these vehicle and energy sales translated to revenues, profits, and cash flow.

What's next for Tesla

Tesla will still have a little sales momentum in the short term. The $7,500 U.S. EV tax credit is completely gone for leases, but for purchases anyone who got their order in with a down payment before the end of September can still get it. Thus, those sales should continue for a few more weeks. After that, expectations call for a sales drop in the U.S., so we'll see what Tesla does with its pricing to get demand back up. We are also expecting the launch of at least one more affordable vehicle before the end of the year. In China, most of the incentives the company has offered in recent months have been extended through the end of October.

The second major item will be the progress of Tesla's robotaxi platform. Full self-driving version 14 is expected to be released soon, with public access expanding over time. On the Q2 conference call, CEO Elon Musk said that he believed Tesla would have autonomous ride-hailing for half of the U.S. population by the end of the year. At the moment, however, Tesla only offers the service in a handful of cities and they have safety drivers in the vehicle to take over if needed. Thus, the latest Elon Musk prediction on autonomy seems likely to be missed, after many years of delays already.

A look at current valuations

When you look at Tesla, the stock itself looks extremely expensive for an automotive company. The stock finished Wednesday at about 190 times its expected adjusted earnings for 2026. As a comparison, the big two traditional U.S. automakers' average numbers are in the single digits times their respective expected earnings. Even if you factor in Tesla as a part-tech company, the valuation is still many times that of the major U.S. tech giants, which mostly currently go for 20 to 30 times their respective annual earnings.

Furthermore, in the chart below, you can see how Tesla matches up on a price-to-expected sales basis for 2026 vs. other EV names: Lucid Group, Inc. (LCID), Rivian Automotive, Inc. (RIVN), and VinFast Auto Ltd. (VFS), XPeng Inc. (XPEV), Polestar Automotive Holding UK PLC (PSNY), BYD Company Limited (OTCPK:BYDDF), NIO Inc. (NIO), and ZEEKR Intelligent Technology Holding Limited (ZK). All valuations are as of Wednesday's close.

The average of the eight comparison names is 1.41 times their expected revenues for next year. A number of these other companies are projected to see much higher revenue growth rates than Tesla in the short term. Of course, they are all working off much smaller base numbers. Additionally, Tesla is the only one of these names that delivers any meaningful amount of profitability and cash flow currently, outside of BYD at times, but the premium that shares trade for is massive. As of Wednesday's close, the average price target on the Street was about $345, which represented almost 25% downside from the day's finish. However, of the 45 analysts covering the name, 20 had some kind of Buy rating, with 17 Holds, against just 8 sells.

Final thoughts and recommendation

Tesla announced a quarterly delivery record on Thursday, taking advantage of the expiring tax credit in the United States. Shares had been rallying considerably into the report, so there wasn't a massive move after the news in pre-market trading. Now, investors will see how much of a delivery falloff there is in Q4 and how the company adjusts pricing to respond to the potential demand drop.

Given Tesla is near its all-time high and results moving forward should be a little weaker, this might be a good time to sell into strength. The valuation at this point is well above all other names in the space, and the robotaxi plan is moving extremely slowly at this point. Tesla has a lot to prove in 2026, and given how sharp the rally has been recently, it wouldn't be a shock to see a pullback occur.

data.title: Cyclical Outlook October 2025: Tariffs, Technology, And Transition
data.description:
Economic outlook takeaways

Clashing forces create winners and losers

A growing tension among three macro forces - trade frictions, the AI investment boom, and challenges to institutions including the Federal Reserve - could test conventional economic and investment frameworks, drive volatility, and widen the gap between winners and losers, both in the U.S. and globally.

Delayed tariff effects begin to bite

Economic growth has been surprisingly resilient, but that appears likely to change. After preemptive actions boosted global trade flows and goods production, many countries now face a transition, with mounting pressure from tariffs and constrained fiscal flexibility. In the U.S., we believe the main risk from tariffs isn’t a price adjustment - it’s that unemployment could rise. The Fed and other central banks have ample room for more interest rate cuts.

Tech investment provides support amid signs of weakness

Global data trends point to a weaker period ahead before targeted fiscal stimulus in some regions starts to kick in. At the same time, resilient tech investment, especially in the U.S. and China, is poised to continue, with potentially growing effects on productivity and the labor market.

Investment outlook takeaways

Bond yields offer durable opportunities, while cash rates are poised to decline

Locking in today’s attractive starting bond yields can support strong returns and income potential in the years ahead across a variety of economic scenarios. With rates on cash-like investments likely to decline alongside central bank policy rates, we expect bonds to outperform. We favor short and intermediate bond maturities.

Global diversification can enhance outperformance potential

Investors can take advantage of today’s unusual abundance of global fixed income opportunities, with attractive real and nominal yields available in a variety of countries. Diversification across regions and currencies is an effective way to fortify portfolios and harvest sources of return.

Relative value can be a guide across the public-private credit continuum

The conventional divide between public and private credit is giving way to a more integrated view. We see a continuum of opportunity spanning across these markets that should be evaluated on differences in liquidity and economic sensitivity. We focus on high quality assets and see strong return potential in asset-based finance.

Economic outlook: A clash of forces tests conventional frameworks

The Trump administration aims to reshape the U.S.’s global role while improving the country’s trade balance. In previous Cyclical Outlooks, we argued that addressing these imbalances would require difficult-to-implement reforms in both the U.S. and its trading partners (for more, see our April 2025 Cyclical Outlook, “Seeking Stability”).

Since our last Cyclical Forum in March, the administration has enacted sweeping overhauls. The impact on the trade balance remains uncertain. However, we believe three forces - tariff effects, the technology investment boom, and challenges to institutions - will likely drive greater economic and capital market volatility within the U.S. and globally (for more, see our June 2025 Secular Outlook, “The Fragmentation Era”).

Tariff effects set to bite

Since President Donald Trump’s term began in January, the U.S. has raised tariffs on every major trading partner. The result has been the largest effective average U.S. tariff rate increase in over a century - from under 3% in 2024 to about 11% as of September 2025, according to the U.S. International Trade Commission. Tariffs remain an administration priority even as legal challenges could delay or disrupt implementation.

Trade theory suggests that U.S. tariffs tend to raise U.S. import prices, depress foreign export prices, reduce real trade volumes, and weigh on real incomes globally. So far, that hasn’t happened. Global growth in trade flows and goods production has accelerated. Global goods inflation has firmed while U.S. inflation has been contained.

Nevertheless, there are reasons to believe that we may be nearing a transition, and that what has been a mini boom could give way to a mini bust:

  • First, consumers and businesses accelerated activity earlier this year to front-run tariffs. The inventory buildup boosted global industrial production and trade (see Figure 1). Now that tariffs have been implemented, accelerated goods production could give way to a period of weak growth or contraction.

Figure 1: Global industrial production and exports to the U.S. spiked ahead of tariffs

  • Second, high effective tariffs have not suppressed Chinese production and trade. Instead, they initially stimulated growth in Southeast Asian economies that are now intermediating more trade to the U.S. The U.S. is cracking down through additional tariffs on goods routed through connector countries.
  • Third, rather than primarily raising prices, many U.S. companies appear to be focused on cost management and gaining market share, with a potential pickup in layoffs from small and midsize businesses that can’t pass on additional costs.

The outlook improves in 2026. U.S. households and businesses will likely benefit from new tax cuts and credits. In countries such as Germany, China, Japan, and Canada, we expect targeted fiscal easing - including infrastructure investment, defense spending, and tax cuts - to offset some drag from U.S. trade policy.

In countries with tighter fiscal constraints, the burden will fall more heavily on central banks. Those with high trade exposure and elevated policy rates - such as Brazil, Mexico, and South Africa - are likely to cut rates more aggressively, especially if the trade-weighted U.S. dollar continues to weaken.

The AI investment boom rolls on

Technology investment continues to power U.S. economic resilience and seemingly boundless equity market performance. AI-related capital spending (see Figure 2) will likely remain a driver of U.S. investment growth through 2026. With AI adoption broadening, investment in infrastructure including data centers and specialized chips will likely remain robust. China is also aggressively building out AI infrastructure with government incentives and industry adoption targets.

Figure 2: AI-driven capital spending has been a major contributor to real U.S. GDP growth

Source: U.S. Commerce Department, Haver Analytics, and PIMCO calculations as of 30 June 2025

Technology is also starting to reshape labor markets. Large firms with the resources to invest in AI can reduce reliance on labor while gaining market share. Tech firms have already reduced hiring for entry level positions, with unemployment rising for people aged 16-25, including college graduates.

Challenges to institutions contribute to uncertainty

Trump administration actions are reshaping traditional institutions, including the Fed. In August, President Trump dismissed Fed Governor Lisa Cook on allegations of mortgage fraud. The case is being litigated, but it signals that President Trump may seek to rebalance the Fed Board of Governors toward his policy preferences - and to do so before the terms of Chair Jerome Powell and all regional Fed bank presidents expire in 2026.

There are good reasons to believe the Fed will continue to operate as an institution independent of short-term political influence. Markets are pricing a policy rate near 3%, in line with estimates of neutral interest rates, but a key risk scenario is a potential Trump administration reshaping of the Fed’s leadership.

Paths for economic growth, inflation, and monetary policy set to vary

In Europe, U.S. demands related to defense spending have prompted renewed commitments from NATO allies while straining budgets. Germany’s planned fiscal expansion is focused on greater defense and infrastructure investment, with implications for its debt trajectory and broader EU fiscal coordination.

Other eurozone economies have less flexibility and will likely offset defense investments with tighter policy elsewhere. These trends will further complicate France’s fiscal challenges, which require more meaningful reforms.

Globally, growth appears to be peaking. We expect it to slow in 2025 as tariffs trigger adjustments. As a baseline, these adjustments can occur without recession and with growth returning to a trend-like 3% pace in 2026. However, near-term risks are tilted to the downside as front-loading has masked weakness.

Chinese growth is already cooling. Trade pressures and domestic challenges are being partially offset by government support, but more is likely needed. In emerging markets (EM), weaker growth and stronger currencies create significant room for rate cuts amid trade shocks, limited fiscal flexibility, and slower monetary transmission.

Global inflation should remain generally benign through 2026, with regional divergence. Without a currency adjustment, tariffs should result in a relative price adjustment between the U.S. and the rest of the world.

The U.S. will likely remain a laggard in reaching its 2% inflation target. Inflation in developed markets (DM) excluding the U.S. is likely to converge to 2% central bank target levels by 2026. Excess capacity should keep Chinese inflation near zero, while China’s exports depress prices abroad as it finds new markets for goods previously sold in the U.S. In EM, inflation will stay within central bank comfort zones, with a risk of undershooting if currencies strengthen, in our view.

Globally, monetary easing is set to continue. The Bank of England and Reserve Bank of Australia are likely to cut more aggressively as disinflation resumes, while the European Central Bank and Bank of Canada - which are closer to neutral policy levels - will make smaller adjustments. The Bank of Japan remains an exception, with below-neutral policy and a rate hike anticipated. Central banks have room to cut rates more than is currently priced into markets if U.S. tariff fallout worsens and fiscal easing proves an insufficient offset.

The Fed must balance tighter immigration policy, AI-driven labor displacement, and tariff-related shocks. In the near term, a key question is whether labor market risks materialize and raise unemployment.

Over the next few years, it remains to be seen whether productivity gains from AI and automation can offset immigration-related labor supply shocks, with fiscal policy in 2026 providing more support. If productivity doesn’t accelerate, recovering economic demand amid constrained supply could lead to more persistent inflation - a tough environment for any Fed chair.

Investment implications: Take advantage of durable opportunities

Locking in today’s attractive bond yields presents a compelling opportunity to support income, returns, and potential price appreciation in the years ahead across a variety of economic scenarios. The fixed income opportunity is especially timely with central banks globally poised to cut interest rates further.

Starting yields have historically been a strong predictor of subsequent five-year returns. Looking at high quality bond benchmarks as of 26 September 2025, the Bloomberg US Aggregate Index yield is 4.42% and the Global Aggregate Index (U.S. dollar hedged) yield is 4.73%. From this baseline, active managers can seek to construct portfolios potentially yielding about 5%-7% by capitalizing on attractive yields available in high grade investments.

Amid ongoing policy uncertainty, we must consider a range of possible outcomes. It makes sense to focus on a diversified set of investments and to prioritize portfolio resilience. Fixed income valuations are attractive both in absolute terms and relative to equities, which have climbed to historically lofty levels. Bond allocations remain an anchor for investment portfolios, providing stability and a potential hedge against elevated equity market risks.

As central bank policy rate cuts continue, steepness is returning to the front end of bond yield curves. Bonds appear poised to outperform cash, while active management can improve outcomes through yield curve positioning.

Rates, duration exposure, and bond yield curve positioning

Even after the strong year-to-date performance for bonds, yields on U.S. 10-year Treasuries remain well within the 3.75%-4.75% range that’s served as an anchoring reference point over the past couple of years (see Figure 3). Forward curves generally price central banks returning to the range of neutral policy rates - although with the U.K. an important exception, with the market still pricing in a terminal rate well above our neutral estimate range.

Figure 3: Global yields remain attractive

Against this backdrop, investors with exposure to duration - a gauge of price sensitivity to changes in interest rates, which tends to be higher in longer-dated bonds - have seen strong performance this year. Positions that benefit from a steepening yield curve have also delivered solid returns.

At this point, we retain an overall bias toward being overweight duration, with a tilt toward U.S. duration and selective exposure in the U.K. and Australia, although with somewhat less conviction than earlier this year given yields have moved lower within our reference range. We favor short and intermediate maturities across global markets, and we are overweight the five-year area in the U.S., as a hedge against downside risks.

We retain our curve-steepening bias but with reduced conviction. Our focus is on potential bull steepening via front-end rallies, rather than bear steepening from long-end selloffs.

Global opportunities

Diversification across regions and currencies is an increasingly important way to tap into potential sources of outperformance. Investors can take advantage of today’s unusually attractive array of global opportunities.

We favor a continued underweight to the U.S. dollar, although we still don’t forecast a shift in its status as the world’s reserve currency. Given risks to the U.S. outlook, including rising deficits, we believe diversifying positions across global markets makes sense. In EM local debt, we favor being overweight duration in Peru and South Africa.

Real assets can serve as a hedge against inflation uncertainty. High real yields and muted inflation expectations embedded in U.S. Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (TIPS) prices make them an affordable hedge against inflation shocks. Commodities can further improve inflation hedging and diversification.

Credit

We see solid fundamentals in the corporate credit sector but believe other fixed income segments offer better risk/reward profiles. We maintain limited exposure to corporate credit amid tight spreads and economic uncertainty. We favor senior structured credit and investments linked to higher-quality consumers. We advise caution in economically sensitive sectors - especially those connected to trade - with high leverage and disruption risks.

We retain an overweight to structured credit and the investment grade credit derivatives index (IG CDX) combined with an underweight to cash corporate credit. We are overweight agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS), with a preference for higher coupons.

We continue to seek relative value across credit markets. Rather than focusing on arbitrary distinctions between public and private credit, we see a continuum of investment opportunities across these markets that should be evaluated on comparisons of liquidity and economic sensitivity.

We focus on liquid, high quality assets and see strong return potential in asset-based finance. We also favor investment themes with secular tailwinds. These include aviation finance and data infrastructure, where capital needs are large and growing, collateral fundamentals are strong, and barriers to entry for lenders are high. Finally, we also are excited to capitalize on select areas where valuations have already reset - notably real estate debt opportunities secured by high quality assets - and in sectors with resilient fundamentals.

Conclusion

In today’s complicated global environment, active managers can use a variety of tools to access broad-based opportunities. Attractive bond yields present a compelling long-term opportunity - particularly as central bank rate cuts boost the potential for fixed income total returns and diminish the potential returns for cash-like investments.

Additionally, global diversification and a more integrated view of public and private credit markets offer ways to boost portfolio resilience and expand sources of return. Active investors can access the abundance of real and nominal yields across regions and currencies, while evaluating credit opportunities along a continuum based on liquidity and economic sensitivity.

Together, these strategies - bond yield capture, global diversification, and credit continuum analysis - can form a robust investment framework.
